title: CLAUDE.md for medrobust Package
text: Smoke-testing differential-misclassification simulations surfaced two real bugs, now fixed verified. manuscript §4.2 derivation verified exact (population recovery 5e-17); faults implementation. bound_ne_mediator.R mediator SOLVE — mis-specified 3×3 system (whose P01 row used Y=1 parameterization (1-pi)*g0 instead Y=0 form (1-pi)*(1-g0)) replaced two per-Y-stratum 2×2 systems (solvable iff Sn_y+Sp_y≠1). Point test moved 1.601→1.495 (→1.480 n→∞; residual finite-sample, confirmed n-scaling sweep). bound_ne_exposure.R audited correct (closed-form 2×2 inverse, verified 1e-16) — never affected. compute_true_effects() (simulate_dm_data.R) — replaced plug--mean-M/mean-C Monte-Carlo g-computation empirical confounder distribution. @true_effects now returns NDE_OR=1.48025 / NIE_OR=1.19940 = oracle (1.500). odds_to_prob() (utilities_helpers.R) — now maps infinite odds → probability 1, perfect classification (sn=1/sp=1) longer yields NaN. compute_effects_from_params() (utilities_helpers.R) CORRECT — changed (verified: fed true params returns oracle). Verification status (green): - devtools::test(): 157 pass / 0 fail / 1 skip (incl. new test-recovery.R, test-true-effects.R, test-bound-contains-truth.R). - devtools::check() (---cran): 0 errors / 0 warnings / 2 benign NOTEs (new submission, dev-version string). - New vignette vignettes/identification-math.qmd documents derivation; registered _pkgdown.yml. Authoritative docs: PLAN-fix-bound_ne-solve-2026-06-11.md, ISSUE-true-effects-estimand-2026-06-11.md, START--fix-true-effects.md; reference oracles dev-diagnostics/ (gitignored). Downstream: regenerate manuscript M2a/M2b illustrative numbers scale sims (n_grid≥50) merge. Remaining: PR fix/true-effects-estimand → main. (Merged via PR #2, 2026-06-11.)

what: ✅ RESOLVED — exposure NIE bound fixed (2026-06-11, branch fix/exposure-nie)
title: CLAUDE.md for medrobust Package
text: exposure (*) path’s NIE bound missed truth (true 1.199 vs [0.980, 0.991]) NDE bound correct. Root cause: bound_ne_exposure.R recovers conditional P(=| M,Y,C), compute_effects_from_joint_probs() consumed joint P(,M,Y | C), dropping observed P(M,Y | C) weight → M,Y marginal became effectively uniform → P(M|=1) P(M|=0) collapsed toward shape → NIE driven null (NDE survives fixes mediator distribution M(0) terms). Fix: multiply recovered conditional observed P(M=m, Y=y | C) (M Y misclassified exposure scenario) form joint, evaluate_param_set() (R/bound_ne_exposure.R, shared serial parallel paths). exposure solve already correct; NIE assembly wrong. compute_effects_from_joint_probs() otherwise correct changed structurally. Verified: point test dev-diagnostics/bne_point_test_exposure.R recovers NDE 1.480 / NIE 1.199 within 0.01 (NIE ~0.99); smoke2_popcheck_both_paths.R exposure NIE row now TRUE; new tests test-recovery-exposure.R, test-bound-contains-truth-exposure.R pass; mediator path unaffected. Oracle: dev-diagnostics/oracle_exposure.R.

title: Expanding Formula Interface: Feasibility Analysis
text: interaction, mediation effect becomes heterogeneous: M=α0+α1X+α2C+ϵMY=β0+β1X+β2M+β3(X×M)+β4C+ϵY \begin{align} M &= \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 X + \alpha_2 C + \epsilon_M \\ Y &= \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 M + \beta_3 (X \times M) + \beta_4 C + \epsilon_Y \end{align} Key issue: PmedP_{\text{med}} now depends XX level: Pmed(x*,x∣X=x0)=P(Yx*,Mx(x0)>Yx,Mx(x0)) P_{\text{med}}(x^*, x \mid X = x_0) = P(Y_{x^*, M_x}(x_0) > Y_{x, M_x}(x_0)) x0x_0 conditioning value.

previous_headings: Extension 1: Exposure-Mediator Interactions > API Design Options
title: Expanding Formula Interface: Feasibility Analysis
text: Implementation sketch: PROS: - ✓ Natural R syntax (users already know X:M) - ✓ Backward compatible (interaction = standard P_med) - ✓ Clear error messages guide user - ✓ Flexible conditioning options CONS: - ✗ Requires parsing formula terms (moderate complexity) - ✗ Users must understand conditioning means
code:
# User specifies interaction in formula (standard R syntax) pmed(   formula_y = Y ~ X + M + X:M + C,   formula_m = M ~ X + C,   data = data,   treatment = "X",   mediator = "M",      # New argument for interactions   condition_on_x = 0,  # Evaluate P_med at X = 0   # OR   condition_on_x = "mean",  # Marginalize over X distribution   # OR   condition_on_x = c(0, 1),  # Multiple conditioning values ) pmed.formula <- function(formula_y, formula_m, data, treatment, mediator,                          condition_on_x = NULL,                          ...) {      # Parse formula_y   terms_y <- stats::terms(formula_y)      # Check for interaction   has_interaction <- .detect_interaction(terms_y, treatment, mediator)      if (has_interaction) {     # Interaction detected     if (is.null(condition_on_x)) {       stop(         "Interaction detected between treatment and mediator.
",         "Please specify 'condition_on_x' to evaluate P_med:
",         "  - Numeric value (e.g., 0, 1)
",         "  - 'mean' to marginalize over X distribution
",         "  - Vector for multiple conditioning values"       )     }          # Compute P_med with interaction     result <- .pmed_interaction(       formula_y = formula_y,       formula_m = formula_m,       data = data,       treatment = treatment,       mediator = mediator,       condition_on_x = condition_on_x,       ...     )        } else {     # No interaction - standard P_med     result <- .pmed_standard(...)   }      return(result) }  .detect_interaction <- function(terms_obj, treatment, mediator) {   # Check if X:M or M:X appears in model terms   factors <- attr(terms_obj, "factors")   factor_names <- rownames(factors)      # Interaction appears as "X:M" or "M:X"   interaction_pattern <- paste0(     c(       paste(treatment, mediator, sep = ":"),       paste(mediator, treatment, sep = ":")     ),     collapse = "|"   )      any(grepl(interaction_pattern, factor_names)) }

title: Expanding Formula Interface: Feasibility Analysis
text: PROS: - ✓ Clear separation concerns - ✓ Simpler implement initially CONS: - ✗ Users must know function call - ✗ Less elegant (two functions similar things)
code: # Different function for interaction models pmed_interaction(   formula_y = Y ~ X + M + X:M + C,   formula_m = M ~ X + C,   data = data,   treatment = "X",   mediator = "M",   x_levels = c(0, 1)  # Required )

title: Expanding Formula Interface: Feasibility Analysis
code: #' Compute P_med with X-M Interaction #' #' @keywords internal .pmed_interaction <- function(formula_y, formula_m, data, treatment, mediator,                               condition_on_x, ...) {      # Fit models   fit_m <- stats::glm(formula_m, data = data, ...)   fit_y <- stats::glm(formula_y, data = data, ...)      if (is.numeric(condition_on_x) && length(condition_on_x) == 1) {     # Single conditioning value     .pmed_interaction_fixed_x(fit_m, fit_y, data, treatment, mediator,                               x_level = condition_on_x, ...)        } else if (condition_on_x == "mean") {     # Marginalize over X distribution     .pmed_interaction_marginalized(fit_m, fit_y, data, treatment, mediator, ...)        } else if (is.numeric(condition_on_x)) {     # Multiple conditioning values     purrr::map(condition_on_x, function(x_val) {       .pmed_interaction_fixed_x(fit_m, fit_y, data, treatment, mediator,                                x_level = x_val, ...)     })   } }  #' P_med at Fixed X Level #' #' @keywords internal .pmed_interaction_fixed_x <- function(fit_m, fit_y, data, treatment, mediator,                                       x_level, n_sim = 10000, ...) {      # Extract coefficients   coefs_m <- stats::coef(fit_m)   coefs_y <- stats::coef(fit_y)      # For each observation, compute counterfactuals at X = x_level   n <- nrow(data)      # Generate M_x (mediator under treatment)   data_x <- data   data_x[[treatment]] <- 1   m_x <- stats::predict(fit_m, newdata = data_x, type = "response")      # Generate M_x* (mediator under control)     data_xref <- data   data_xref[[treatment]] <- 0   m_xref <- stats::predict(fit_m, newdata = data_xref, type = "response")      # For each simulated M, compute Y counterfactuals   # Critical: X is FIXED at x_level for Y model      y_1_mx <- numeric(n)   y_0_mx <- numeric(n)      for (i in seq_len(n)) {     # Sample M_x from distribution     m_sample <- stats::rnorm(n_sim, mean = m_x[i], sd = stats::sigma(fit_m))          # Y(X=1, M=M_x) with X fixed at x_level     data_y <- data[i, , drop = FALSE]     data_y[[treatment]] <- x_level  # CONDITIONING     data_y[[mediator]] <- m_sample          y_1_mx[i] <- mean(stats::predict(fit_y, newdata = data_y, type = "response"))          # Y(X=0, M=M_x) with X fixed at x_level     data_y[[treatment]] <- x_level  # CONDITIONING (same)     m_sample_ref <- stats::rnorm(n_sim, mean = m_xref[i], sd = stats::sigma(fit_m))     data_y[[mediator]] <- m_sample_ref          y_0_mx[i] <- mean(stats::predict(fit_y, newdata = data_y, type = "response"))   }      # P_med = P(Y_1,Mx > Y_0,Mx | X = x_level)   pmed <- mean(y_1_mx > y_0_mx)      return(pmed) }

title: Expanding Formula Interface: Feasibility Analysis
text: Two scenarios handle: Parallel mediators: M1, M2 independently mediate X → Y Sequential mediators: X → M1 → M2 → Y

previous_headings: Extension 2: Multiple Mediators > API Design Options
what: Option A: List of Formulas ⭐ RECOMMENDED for Parallel
title: Expanding Formula Interface: Feasibility Analysis
text: Implementation: Total Indirect Effect (M1 M2): Pmedtotal=P(Yx*,M1x,M2x>Yx,M1x,M2x) P_{\text{med}}^{\text{total}} = P(Y_{x^*, M1_x, M2_x} > Y_{x, M1_x, M2_x}) Specific Indirect Effect (M1 , holding M2 control): PmedM1=P(Yx*,M1x,M2x*>Yx*,M1x*,M2x*) P_{\text{med}}^{M1} = P(Y_{x^*, M1_x, M2_{x^*}} > Y_{x^*, M1_{x^*}, M2_{x^*}})
code: # Parallel mediators pmed(   formula_y = Y ~ X + M1 + M2 + C,   formula_m = list(     M1 ~ X + C,     M2 ~ X + C   ),   data = data,   treatment = "X",   mediator = c("M1", "M2"),   effect = "total"  # Total indirect effect through both )  # Specific indirect effect through M1 only pmed(   formula_y = Y ~ X + M1 + M2 + C,   formula_m = list(     M1 ~ X + C,     M2 ~ X + C   ),   data = data,   treatment = "X",   mediator = "M1",  # Focus on M1   fix_other_mediators = "control"  # Hold M2 at control level ) pmed.formula <- function(formula_y, formula_m, data, treatment, mediator,                          effect = c("total", "specific"),                          fix_other_mediators = c("control", "treatment"),                          ...) {      effect <- match.arg(effect)   fix_other <- match.arg(fix_other_mediators)      # Check if formula_m is a list (multiple mediators)   if (is.list(formula_m)) {     # Multiple mediators detected          if (effect == "total") {       # Total indirect effect through all mediators       .pmed_multiple_total(         formula_y, formula_m, data, treatment, mediator, ...       )            } else if (effect == "specific") {       # Specific indirect effect through one mediator       if (length(mediator) > 1) {         stop("For specific effects, provide single mediator name")       }              .pmed_multiple_specific(         formula_y, formula_m, data, treatment, mediator,         fix_other = fix_other, ...       )     }        } else {     # Single mediator - standard P_med     .pmed_standard(...)   } }

title: Expanding Formula Interface: Feasibility Analysis
text: Mathematical Definition (Path-Specific Effect): Following VanderWeele & Vansteelandt (2013), path-specific indirect effect M1 → M2: PmedM1→M2=P(Yx*,M1x,M2(x*,M1x)>Yx,M1x,M2(x,M1x)) P_{\text{med}}^{M1 \M2} = P(Y_{x^*, M1_x, M2(x^*, M1_x)} > Y_{x, M1_x, M2(x, M1_x)}) requires nested counterfactuals (M2 depends counterfactual M1).
code: # Sequential mediation: X → M1 → M2 → Y pmed_sequential(   formulas = list(     M1 ~ X + C,     M2 ~ X + M1 + C,     Y ~ X + M1 + M2 + C   ),   data = data,   path = c("X", "M1", "M2", "Y")  # Specify causal path )  # Or more explicitly: pmed_path_specific(   formulas = list(...),   data = data,   from = "X",   through = c("M1", "M2"),  # Sequential chain   to = "Y" )

title: Expanding Formula Interface: Feasibility Analysis
code: #' P_med with Multiple Parallel Mediators (Total Effect) #' #' @keywords internal .pmed_multiple_total <- function(formula_y, formula_m_list, data, treatment,                                  mediators, n_sim = 10000, ...) {      # Fit mediator models   fits_m <- purrr::map(formula_m_list, ~stats::glm(.x, data = data, ...))      # Fit outcome model   fit_y <- stats::glm(formula_y, data = data, ...)      n <- nrow(data)      # For each observation, simulate counterfactuals   y_1_m1x_m2x <- numeric(n)   y_0_m1x_m2x <- numeric(n)      for (i in seq_len(n)) {     # Generate M1_x, M2_x (mediators under treatment)     data_x <- data[i, , drop = FALSE]     data_x[[treatment]] <- 1          m_x_samples <- purrr::map(fits_m, function(fit) {       mu <- stats::predict(fit, newdata = data_x, type = "response")       stats::rnorm(n_sim, mean = mu, sd = stats::sigma(fit))     })          # Generate M1_x*, M2_x* (mediators under control)     data_xref <- data[i, , drop = FALSE]     data_xref[[treatment]] <- 0          m_xref_samples <- purrr::map(fits_m, function(fit) {       mu <- stats::predict(fit, newdata = data_xref, type = "response")       stats::rnorm(n_sim, mean = mu, sd = stats::sigma(fit))     })          # Compute Y counterfactuals     # Y(X=1, M1=M1_x, M2=M2_x)     data_y_1 <- data[i, , drop = FALSE]     data_y_1[[treatment]] <- 1     for (j in seq_along(mediators)) {       data_y_1[[mediators[j]]] <- m_x_samples[[j]]     }     y_1_m1x_m2x[i] <- mean(stats::predict(fit_y, newdata = data_y_1, type = "response"))          # Y(X=0, M1=M1_x, M2=M2_x)     data_y_0 <- data[i, , drop = FALSE]     data_y_0[[treatment]] <- 0     for (j in seq_along(mediators)) {       data_y_0[[mediators[j]]] <- m_x_samples[[j]]  # Same M as treatment     }     y_0_m1x_m2x[i] <- mean(stats::predict(fit_y, newdata = data_y_0, type = "response"))   }      # P_med = P(Y_1,M1x,M2x > Y_0,M1x,M2x)   pmed <- mean(y_1_m1x_m2x > y_0_m1x_m2x)      return(pmed) }

title: Expanding Formula Interface: Feasibility Analysis
text: existing medrobust package can naturally extend handle: Implementation medrobust:
code: library(probmed) library(medrobust)  # Compute P_med result <- pmed(   formula_y = Y ~ X + M + C,   formula_m = M ~ X + C,   data = data )  # Sensitivity analysis using medrobust sens <- sensitivity_analysis(   result,   rho_range = seq(-0.5, 0.5, by = 0.1),   method = "pmed"  # New method for medrobust )  plot(sens) # Add S7 method for PmedResult S7::method(sensitivity_analysis, PmedResult) <- function(object,                                                           rho_range,                                                           ...) {   # Extract from PmedResult   a <- object@source_extract@a_path   b <- object@source_extract@b_path      # Compute P_med under confounding   pmed_sens <- purrr::map_dbl(rho_range, function(rho) {     .compute_pmed_with_confounding(       a = a, b = b,       rho = rho,       data = object@source_extract@data     )   })      # Return sensitivity object   structure(     list(       rho = rho_range,       pmed = pmed_sens,       original = object@estimate,       threshold = .find_tipping_point(rho_range, pmed_sens)     ),     class = c("pmed_sensitivity", "sensitivity")   ) }

title: Advanced Grid Search Algorithms for bound_ne()
text: Strategy: Exhaustive regular grid 4 parameters Evaluations: n_grid^4 (e.g., 10^4 = 10,000) Use : Need exact coverage, small grids (n_grid ≤ 5) Speed: Baseline (1x)

title: Advanced Grid Search Algorithms for bound_ne()
text: Strategy: Coarse grid → fine grid refinement compatible regions Evaluations: ~1-20% full grid falsification high Use : Sparse compatible regions, n_grid ≥ 10 Speed: 5-10x faster (depends falsification rate)

title: Advanced Grid Search Algorithms for bound_ne()
text: Strategy: Space-filling design ensuring uniform coverage Evaluations: sqrt(n_grid^4) ≈ 100 n_grid=10 Use : Exploration, need broad coverage quickly Speed: 50-100x faster, 99% fewer evaluations Accuracy: Bounds typically within 10-30% width exact

title: Advanced Grid Search Algorithms for bound_ne()
text: Strategy: Low-discrepancy quasi-random sequences Evaluations: sqrt(n_grid^4) ≈ 100 n_grid=10 Use : High-dimensional parameter spaces Speed: 50-100x faster Accuracy: Similar LHS, slightly better >4 dimensions

title: Advanced Grid Search Algorithms for bound_ne()
text: Strategy: Find exact boundaries compatible/incompatible Evaluations: O(log n) per dimension monotonic Use : Compatibility monotonic parameters Speed: 10-50x faster (depends monotonicity) Accuracy: Excellent boundaries, may miss interior structure

title: Advanced Grid Search Algorithms for bound_ne()
text: works: 1. Divide parameter range N equal intervals 2. Random sample within interval 3. Randomly permute assignments across dimensions 4. Result: Space-filling design guaranteed coverage Advantages: - Guarantees coverage entire parameter space - Much efficient random sampling - Robust across different problem types Limitations: - Approximate bounds (exact extremes) - May miss narrow compatible regions

title: Advanced Grid Search Algorithms for bound_ne()
text: works: 1. Generate low-discrepancy quasi-random sequence 2. Ensures even coverage minimal clumping 3. Better random sampling multi-dimensional spaces Advantages: - Better uniformity LHS >4 dimensions - Deterministic (reproducible without seed) Limitations: - Similar LHS 4D - Slightly complex implement

title: Advanced Grid Search Algorithms for bound_ne()
text: works: 1. Test corner points parameter space 2. parameter, binary search find boundaries 3. Sample densely near boundaries (Beta distribution) 4. Focused finding exact bound edges Advantages: - efficient compatibility monotonic - Finds exact boundaries quickly Limitations: - Assumes monotonicity (always true) - May miss complex interior structures - Falls back LHS assumption fails

title: BUG / ISSUE — compute_true_effects() uses plug-in-mean-M, not g-computation
text: compute_true_effects() (R/simulate_dm_data.R) computes natural direct/indirect effects substituting mediator probability prob_M_a outcome model’s linear predictor: natural-effect estimand. binary mediator correct g-computation averages outcome M distribution: expit nonlinear, expit(.. + tMY*E[M] ..) != E_M[ expit(.. + tMY*M ..) ] (Jensen gap). bound_ne() machinery targets correct (averaged) estimand, simulation coverage checked mis-specified truth.
code: prob_M_a1   <- expit(baseline_M + beta_AM*1 + C_mean_M) E_Y_a1_Ma1  <- expit(baseline_Y + theta_AY*1 + theta_MY * prob_M_a1 + ... )  # <-- plug-in mean # E[Y(a, M(a'))] = sum_c P(c) * sum_m P(M=m | a', c) * P(Y=1 | a, m, c) piM <- expit(baseline_M + beta_AM*aprime + beta_C*c) g1  <- expit(baseline_Y + theta_AY*a + theta_MY*1 + theta_C*c) g0  <- expit(baseline_Y + theta_AY*a + theta_MY*0 + theta_C*c) EY  <- piM*g1 + (1 - piM)*g0

what: Secondary issues — RESOLVED into two, after grid×scale sweep (2026-06-11)
title: BUG / ISSUE — compute_true_effects() uses plug-in-mean-M, not g-computation
text: independent base-R analytic bound (manuscript §4.2) run n∈{4k,20k,200k,300k} grid k∈{9,81}. Conclusions: (2a) Grid resolution — usage, bug. Analytic bound MISSES truth coarse grid (k=9) CONTAINS dense grid (k=81, pop scale): NDE [1.4715,1.5300] ∋ 1.4802 ✓. sims must use high n_grid (≥~50–80/axis). (2b) bound_ne g-computation bug — PINPOINTED. Derivation verified exact (popcheck recovers truth 5e-17), fault bound_ne. Direct point test (region = {true Ψ}, n=5e5) returns wrong effect scales: NDE_OR 1.601 / NIE_OR 1.121 vs oracle 1.480 / 1.199; NDE_RD 0.076 / NIE_RD 0.022 vs 0.062 / 0.034. Signature: NDE overstated, NIE understated (opposite directions) → /Jensen conversion (-direction). classic signature using WRONG mediator distribution cross-world g-computation: swap πa\pi_a multiplies γa′\gamma_{'}, M(0)M(0)↔︎M(1)M(1) E[Y(1,M(⋅))]E[Y(1,M(\cdot))]. NDE uses M(0)M(0), NIE M(1)−M(0)M(1)-M(0) contrast, one swap pushes NDE NIE — exactly observed. Fix R/bound_ne_mediator.R (exposure analogue): verify mediator-distribution indices E[Y(,M(*))]E[Y(,M(^*))] assembly dev-diagnostics/popcheck_exact_recovery.R. Repro: dev-diagnostics/{bne_point_test, oracle_potential_outcomes, popcheck_exact_recovery, diag_grid_scale_sweep}.R.

code: n_compatible <- length(results) falsified_proportion <- 1 - (n_compatible / n_total)  # n_total undefined for advanced methods n_compatible <- length(results) if (use_advanced_method) {   # For advanced methods, count how many were actually evaluated   if (grid_method == "lhs" || grid_method == "sobol") {     n_evaluated <- target_samples   } else if (grid_method == "adaptive") {     n_coarse <- ceiling(n_grid / 5)^4     n_evaluated <- n_coarse + n_compatible * 5^4   } else {     n_evaluated <- length(results) + sum(sapply(results, function(x) {       if (!is.null(x$n_evaluated)) x$n_evaluated else 0     }))   } } else {   n_evaluated <- n_total } falsified_proportion <- 1 - (n_compatible / n_evaluated)

what: Analysis & Implementation Plan — finite-sample bias of bound endpoints
title: Analysis & Implementation Plan — finite-sample bias of bound endpoints
text: Study: medrobust partial-ID bound endpoints (NDE/NIE) differential misclassification Branch: feature/analytic-ci Date: 2026-06-12 Version: 1.0 : method-scout (bias-corrected bootstrap [Kilian 1998; SAFE/Nakagawa 2025; Schafer 2024] + Imbens-Manski 2004) → plan

title: Analysis & Implementation Plan — finite-sample bias of bound endpoints
text: Primary: characterize correct finite-sample (downward) bias bound endpoints L̂, Û NDE/NIE CI around bias-corrected endpoints achieves ≈ nominal coverage true effect N ∈ {100, 200, 500}. Estimand (thing measure): bias_n(e) = E[ê_n] − e_pop e ∈ {L, U} × {NDE, NIE}, scales {, RR, RD}, paths {mediator, exposure}. e_pop = endpoint population limit (large-n bound); true effect oracles.

what: 2. Competing hypotheses for the bias source (must discriminate first)
title: Analysis & Implementation Plan — finite-sample bias of bound endpoints
text: H1 — plug-nonlinearity. Bias nonlinear functional (deconvolution ÷(Sn+Sp−1), odds, ratio); shifts θ(Ψ) downward every Ψ. → Predicts bias present even single fixed Ψ (min/max). Supported Schafer (2024): plug-nonlinear-functional estimators biased. H2 — extremum/selection (“winner’s curse”). Bias min/max Ψ-grid; min biased , max biased → widens interval (help, hurt, low-side miss). → Predicts bias appears grid scales grid density × noise. Discriminating test: compare bias single fixed Ψ (degenerate region) vs wide grid. Current evidence (whole bound shifted low) → expect H1 dominant.

title: Analysis & Implementation Plan — finite-sample bias of bound endpoints
text: Truth dev-diagnostics/oracle_potential_outcomes.R, oracle_exposure.R. Grid: path {med, exp} × scale {} (RR/RD) × n {100, 200, 500, 1000, 2000, 8000}, R = 300 reps, fixed Ψ region around truth. endpoint bias mean(L̂) − L_pop, mean(Û) − U_pop (H1 + H2). Output: bias-vs-n table; confirm O(1/n) decay; attribute H1 vs H2. Script: dev-diagnostics/bias_characterization.R.

title: Analysis & Implementation Plan — finite-sample bias of bound endpoints
text: Bias-corrected endpoint via fixed-Ψ envelope resample (already built, ~11× faster full bootstrap): L̂_bc = 2L̂ − mean_b(L̂*_b); Û. Offer median-bias-corrected variant. Imbens–Manski CI around (L̂_bc, Û_bc) envelope SEs. paths (add .effect_at_psi_mediator), scales /RR/RD.

title: Analysis & Implementation Plan — finite-sample bias of bound endpoints
text: N ∈ {100, 200, 500}, ψ ∈ {1, 1.5}, paths, R ≈ 200, B ≈ 150 envelope reps. Metric: CI-envelope coverage true effect; target ≥ 0.93. Report raw-bound vs bias-corrected-CI coverage side side, plus CI width (bias-variance check).

title: Analysis & Implementation Plan — finite-sample bias of bound endpoints
text: Bias-corrected CI ≈ nominal → ship (answer coverage problem). Residual bias (bootstrap correction insufficient deconvolution) → escalate iterated bootstrap [Schafer 2024; Ouysse 2013] analytic 2nd-order [Yang 2015], document asymptotic framing bias curve. Decide evidence, priori.

title: Analysis & Implementation Plan — finite-sample bias of bound endpoints
text: devtools::test() + check() ---cran clean; mediator/exposure point bounds unchanged (regression guard); new tests bias-correction + IM behaviour.

title: Analysis & Implementation Plan — finite-sample bias of bound endpoints
text: Bias correction inflates variance (bias–variance trade-) — monitor CI width + MSE. Envelope SE may understate argmin Ψ moves across resamples — check argmin stability; fall back re-selecting Ψ per resample unstable. Deconvolution near det = Sn+Sp−1 → 0 amplifies bias — exclude/penalize low-det Ψ.

title: Analysis & Implementation Plan — finite-sample bias of bound endpoints
text: R 4.x, pkgload::load_all; fixed seeds; artifacts dev-diagnostics/ (dev-) + tests.

title: Analysis & Implementation Plan — finite-sample bias of bound endpoints
text: Imbens & Manski (2004, Econometrica); Kilian (1998, REStat); Nakagawa et al. (2025, SAFE bootstrap); Schafer (2024, SIMODS, iterated bootstrap / Möbius); Yang (2015, J. Econometrics, 3rd-order bias); Abadie & Imbens (2011, bias-corrected matching).

title: Analysis & Implementation Plan — finite-sample bias of bound endpoints
text: Conclusion: material finite-sample bias correct. -coverage Imbens-Manski narrow-set-vs-sampling-noise problem, IM CI (already built R/bound_ci.R) correct sufficient fix. Evidence: 1. Stage 1 (single-Psi bias, isolates H1): true Psi effect ~unbiased (NDE bias +0.17→+0.01 n=200→8000; NIE +0.02→+0.003) biased high, low. H1 (plug-nonlinearity) cause bound’s apparent low bias. 2. Population bound correct: [0.85,0.95]^2 n=3e5 bound NDE[1.426,1.580] ∋ 1.480 NIE[1.178,1.217] ∋ 1.199; single-Psi corner sweep confirms genuine partial-ID range. earlier “[1.06,1.07]” single unlucky n=2000 realization, systematic bias (-read one sample). 3. Mechanism: identified-set width (~0.15 NDE) comparable endpoint sampling SD small n, raw set -covers true point — textbook IM problem. Raw coverage rises n (0.20→0.83) SD shrinks relative width: IM signature. 4. IM CI achieves nominal coverage (exposure, psi=1, n=500): NIE 0.12→0.93, NDE 0.09→0.95. (dev-diagnostics/im_cov.R.) Revised plan: SKIP bias correction (Stages 2-bias, 4-escalation). Remaining work pure productionization IM CI: mediator primitive + bound_ci_mediator; wire ci_method="analytic" (IM, bias-correction) bound_ne; full coverage table N∈{100,200,500} paths /RR/RD; tests; CRAN-clean; update SPEC-analytic-ci sec 8.

what: PLAN — Fix the bound_ne recovery bug (+ estimand fix)
title: PLAN — Fix the bound_ne recovery bug (+ estimand fix)
text: Repo: medrobust (YES — fix ; research repos consume package). Branch: fix/true-effects-estimand (already checked ; rename/extend OK). Date: 2026-06-11 Status: Root cause identified bugs. Derivation verified exact (popcheck).

what: TL;DR — two bugs, both in medrobust, both root-caused
title: PLAN — Fix the bound_ne recovery bug (+ estimand fix)
text: compute_true_effects() (R/simulate_dm_data.R): plug--mean-M estimand. (Bug #1) 3×3 linear solve bound_ne_mediator.R (lines ~118–125): mis-specified system matrix → recovers wrong (π, γ) → wrong bounds. (Bug #2, ~0.05 offset) compute_effects_from_params() (g-computation) CORRECT — verified: fed true params returns oracle (1.48025/1.19941). touch .

title: PLAN — Fix the bound_ne recovery bug (+ estimand fix)
text: R/bound_ne_mediator.R solves 3×3 system unknown vector theta = (pi*g1, (1-pi)*g0, pi*(1-g1)) matrix: third row encodes P01 = (1-sp0)*theta[2] + sn0*theta[3] = (1-sp0)*(1-pi)*g0 + sn0*pi*(1-g1). true equation (manuscript §4.2) P01 = sn0*(1-g1)*pi + (1-sp0)*(1-g0)*(1-pi). term (1-g0)*(1-pi) theta[2]=(1-pi)*g0. matrix multiplies (1-sp0) WRONG unknown — mixes g0 (Y=1 parameterization) (1-g0) (Y=0) slot. system internally inconsistent ⇒ biased recovery (π, γ) ⇒ NDE↑/NIE↓ offset.

title: PLAN — Fix the bound_ne recovery bug (+ estimand fix)
text: dev-diagnostics/popcheck_exact_recovery.R: CORRECT approach (two 2×2 systems, one per Y) recovers (π,γ) 5e-17 g-computes oracle. dev-diagnostics/instrument.R: g-comp formula TRUE params = oracle; bound_ne n=5e5 data = 1.601/1.121 ⇒ gap SOLVE, formula.

what: The fix (Bug #2) — replace the 3×3 with two clean 2×2 systems
title: PLAN — Fix the bound_ne recovery bug (+ estimand fix)
text: R/bound_ne_mediator.R, replace A_mat/b_vec/theta_sol block (~lines 114–160) per-Y-stratum solve. Observed cells P_ym* fixed (,c): keep existing validity checks (0≤·≤1) boundary handling. Solvable iff det(A1), det(A0) != 0, .e. sn_y + sp_y != 1 — add compatibility pruning. Exposure path — AUDITED 2026-06-11: bug, change. R/bound_ne_exposure.R (lines ~128–135) uses standard 2×2 misclassification matrix inverse P_1 = (sp·P*_1 − (1−sp)·P*_0)/(sn+sp−1), P_0 = (sn·P*_0 − (1−sn)·P*_1)/(sn+sp−1) denom = sn_y+sp_y−1. Verified algebraically correct (recovers true probs 1e-16, incl. differential Sn/Sp). bug SPECIFIC mediator path’s 3×3 formulation. Verification proposed mediator fix (dev-diagnostics/verify_fix.R, 2026-06-11): arbitrary true params DIFFERENTIAL Sn/Sp (Sn1=.88,Sp1=.93,Sn0=.80,Sp0=.85): - current 3×3 recovers pi=0.428/g1=0.344/g0=0.205 (true 0.350/0.420/0.180) → WRONG - two-2×2 fix recovers three 0.00e+00 error, pi consistent across Y blocks → EXACT

title: Power Analysis Optimizations
text: Optimized power_analysis() function significantly better performance eliminating loops improving vectorization.

what: 1. Removed For Loop in Main Function (Line 124)
title: Power Analysis Optimizations
text: : : Benefit: functional approach, easier parallelize future needed, cleaner code.
code: for (n_idx in seq_along(sample_sizes)) {   n <- sample_sizes[n_idx]   # ... run simulations ...   results_list[[n_idx]] <- data.frame(...) } run_one_n <- function(n_idx) {   n <- sample_sizes[n_idx]   # ... run simulations ...   data.frame(...) } results_list <- lapply(seq_along(sample_sizes), run_one_n)

title: Power Analysis Optimizations
text: : : Benefit: - Single operation instead n_sim iterations - ~10-100x faster large n_sim - memory efficient
code: for (i in 1:n_sim) {   if (sim_results[[i]]$success) {     covers_truth[i] <- sim_results[[i]]$covers     rejects_null[i] <- sim_results[[i]]$rejects     widths[i] <- sim_results[[i]]$width     lower_bounds[i] <- sim_results[[i]]$lower     upper_bounds[i] <- sim_results[[i]]$upper   } else {     covers_truth[i] <- NA     # ... etc   } } # Convert list to matrix in one operation results_matrix <- do.call(rbind, sim_results)  # Extract columns (vectorized) covers_truth <- results_matrix[, "covers"] rejects_null <- results_matrix[, "rejects"] widths <- results_matrix[, "width"] lower_bounds <- results_matrix[, "lower"] upper_bounds <- results_matrix[, "upper"] success <- results_matrix[, "success"]

what: 3. Changed Return Type to Named Vector
title: Power Analysis Optimizations
text: : : Benefit: - Named vectors can rbind’ed directly matrices - Enables vectorized extraction - Eliminates need loop extract list lists
code: return(list(   covers = covers,   rejects = rejects,   width = width,   lower = lower,   upper = upper,   success = TRUE )) c(covers = as.numeric(covers),   rejects = as.numeric(rejects),   width = width,   lower = lower,   upper = upper,   success = 1)

code: ## Monte Carlo Simulation  ::: {.callout-note} This example runs a small simulation. Increase `n_reps` for real studies. :::  ```{r} #| cache: true n_reps <- 10  # Use 1000+ for real studies results <- vector("list", n_reps)  for (i in 1:n_reps) {   # Generate data   sim <- simulate_dm_data(     n = 200,     true_params = list(beta_AM = 0.405, theta_AY = 0.405, theta_MY = 0.405),     dm_params = list(sn0 = 0.85, sp0 = 0.85, psi_sn = 1.5, psi_sp = 1.0),     misclass_type = "exposure",     seed = i,     return_truth = FALSE   )    # Compute bounds   b <- bound_ne(     data = sim@observed,     exposure = "A_star",     mediator = "M",     outcome = "Y",     confounders = "C1",     misclassified_variable = "exposure",     sensitivity_region = list(       sn0_range = c(0.80, 0.90),       sp0_range = c(0.80, 0.90),       psi_sn_range = c(1.0, 2.0),       psi_sp_range = c(1.0, 1.0)     ),     n_grid = 20,     verbose = FALSE   )    results[[i]] <- data.frame(     NIE_lower = b@NIE_lower,     NIE_upper = b@NIE_upper,     NDE_lower = b@NDE_lower,     NDE_upper = b@NDE_upper   ) }  # Combine results results_df <- bind_rows(results) summary(results_df)

title: S7 Package Documentation: medrobust
text: document provides comprehensive overview S7 class method architecture medrobust R package.

title: S7 Package Documentation: medrobust
text: medrobust package leverages S7 object-oriented system provide robust type-safe framework sensitivity analysis mediation contexts. architecture centered around key classes: medrobust_bounds: main result container, holding computed partial identification bounds Natural Indirect Effect (NIE) Natural Direct Effect (NDE). encapsulates aspects single analysis run. sensitivity_region: helper class formally defines multi-dimensional parameter space (Θψ) sensitivity analysis performed. compatibility_test: Stores results checking whether specific point sensitivity parameter space compatible observed data. bootstrap_results: Contains results bootstrap inference computed bounds, providing confidence intervals. falsification_summary: Summarizes extent sensitivity parameter space falsified (ruled ) observed data. simulated_dm_data: class hold simulated data known characteristics, essential validation power analysis. power_analysis_result: Stores results power analysis simulation, including power curve sample size recommendations. design emphasizes encapsulation clear separation concerns. medrobust_bounds acts central hub, composing objects like sensitivity_region bootstrap_results. Methods implemented polymorphically print, summary, plot, providing user-friendly ways inspect visualize results.

title: S7 Package Documentation: medrobust

title: S7 Package Documentation: medrobust
text: Purpose: primary container results partial identification analysis. stores calculated bounds NIE NDE, along extensive metadata analysis. Properties: NIE_lower: (numeric) lower bound Natural Indirect Effect. NIE_upper: (numeric) upper bound Natural Indirect Effect. NDE_lower: (numeric) lower bound Natural Direct Effect. NDE_upper: (numeric) upper bound Natural Direct Effect. compatible_sets: (data.frame) data frame row set sensitivity parameters compatible data. n_compatible: (integer) total number compatible parameter sets found. n_evaluated: (integer) total number parameter sets evaluated sensitivity region. falsified_proportion: (numeric) proportion sensitivity parameter space falsified (.e., incompatible data). effect_scale: (character) scale reported effects (e.g., “”, “RR”, “RD”). misclassified_variable: (character) variable assumed misclassified (“exposure” “mediator”). sensitivity_region: (sensitivity_region) S7 object defining parameter space analysis. naive_estimates: (list) list containing NIE NDE calculated without correction misclassification. bootstrap_results: (bootstrap_results | NULL) S7 object containing bootstrap confidence intervals bounds, NULL computed. data_summary: (list) Summary statistics input data. call: (call) function call generated object. Example:
code: # This is a conceptual example. Direct construction is typically done # by the bound_ne() function. bounds_result <- medrobust_bounds(   NIE_lower = 0.1,   NIE_upper = 0.5,   NDE_lower = 0.2,   NDE_upper = 0.8,   compatible_sets = data.frame(sn0 = 0.9, sp0 = 0.9, psi_sn = 1, psi_sp = 1),   n_compatible = 1L,   n_evaluated = 100L,   falsified_proportion = 0.99,   effect_scale = "RD",   misclassified_variable = "exposure",   sensitivity_region = sensitivity_region(     sn0_range = c(0.8, 1), sp0_range = c(0.8, 1),     psi_sn_range = c(1, 1), psi_sp_range = c(1, 1)   ) )

title: S7 Package Documentation: medrobust
text: Purpose: Defines multi-dimensional parameter space sensitivity analysis. specifies plausible range baseline sensitivity/specificity odds ratios differential misclassification. Properties: sn0_range: (numeric) vector length 2 specifying [min, max] range baseline sensitivity (Sn0). sp0_range: (numeric) vector length 2 specifying [min, max] range baseline specificity (Sp0). psi_sn_range: (numeric) vector length 2 specifying [min, max] range odds ratio comparing sensitivity across covariate levels. psi_sp_range: (numeric) vector length 2 specifying [min, max] range odds ratio comparing specificity across covariate levels. Constructor: sensitivity_region() Example:
code: # Define a sensitivity region region <- sensitivity_region(   sn0_range = c(0.85, 0.95),   sp0_range = c(0.80, 0.90),   psi_sn_range = c(0.8, 1.2),   psi_sp_range = c(0.9, 1.1) )

title: S7 Package Documentation: medrobust
text: Purpose: Stores detailed results test single point sensitivity parameter space, indicating whether point compatible observed data. Properties: compatible: (logical) TRUE parameter set compatible, FALSE otherwise. psi: (list) list sensitivity parameters tested (sn0, sp0, psi_sn, psi_sp). violated_constraints: (data.frame) incompatible, data frame detailing constraints violated. reason: (character) string explaining reason incompatibility, applicable. Example:
code: # This is a conceptual example. Direct construction is typically done # by the check_compatibility() function. test_result <- compatibility_test(     compatible = FALSE,     psi = list(sn0 = 0.7, sp0 = 0.7, psi_sn = 1, psi_sp = 1),     reason = "Implied probabilities are not in [0, 1]" )

title: S7 Package Documentation: medrobust
text: Purpose: Encapsulates results bootstrap inference performed partial identification bounds. Properties: method: (character) bootstrap method used (“percentile” “bca”). n_reps: (integer) number bootstrap replications. confidence_level: (numeric) confidence level intervals (e.g., 0.95). nie_lower_ci: (numeric) confidence interval lower bound NIE. nie_upper_ci: (numeric) confidence interval upper bound NIE. nde_lower_ci: (numeric) confidence interval lower bound NDE. nde_upper_ci: (numeric) confidence interval upper bound NDE. Example:
code: # Conceptual example. boot_res <- bootstrap_results(     method = "bca",     n_reps = 1000L,     confidence_level = 0.95,     nie_lower_ci = c(0.05, 0.15),     nie_upper_ci = c(0.45, 0.55),     nde_lower_ci = c(0.15, 0.25),     nde_upper_ci = c(0.75, 0.85) )

title: S7 Package Documentation: medrobust
text: Functionality: Provides concise, user-friendly summary S7 object, suitable display console. Polymorphism: print(medrobust_bounds): Displays calculated NIE/NDE bounds, falsification summary, bootstrap CIs available. print(compatibility_test): Shows tested parameters clearly states whether compatible , providing reason falsification applicable. print(sensitivity_region): Prints defined ranges sensitivity parameter. Example:
code: # Assuming 'bounds_result' is a medrobust_bounds object print(bounds_result)  # Output will be a formatted summary of the bounds.

title: S7 Package Documentation: medrobust
text: Functionality: Provides detailed summary print(), often including additional diagnostic information. Polymorphism: summary(medrobust_bounds): First calls print(), adds details sensitivity region, naive estimates, preview compatible parameter sets. summary(compatibility_test): First calls print(), adds stratum-level details analysis stratified. Example:
code: # Assuming 'bounds_result' is a medrobust_bounds object summary(bounds_result)  # Output will include the print summary plus extra details.

title: S7 Package Documentation: medrobust
text: Functionality: Generates visualization object’s data. Polymorphism: plot(power_analysis_result): Creates multi-panel plot showing power curve, bound width curve, coverage rate function sample size. Requires ggplot2 patchwork gridExtra. Example:
code: # Assuming 'power_res' is a power_analysis_result object # This will generate and display the plots. plot(power_res)

title: S7 Package Documentation: medrobust
text: Functionality: Converts S7 object flat data.frame. Polymorphism: .data.frame(medrobust_bounds): Creates one-row data frame containing key results bounds object, including bounds , widths, bootstrap CI endpoints available. useful programmatically extracting comparing results multiple analyses. Example:
code: # Assuming 'bounds_result' is a medrobust_bounds object df <- as.data.frame(bounds_result)  # 'df' is now a data.frame with columns like NIE_lower, NIE_upper, etc.

title: SPEC (scope) — Fast analytic confidence intervals for bound_ne endpoints
text: Component: R/bound_ne*.R + new R/bound_ci.R (analytic CI) Type: feature (enables finite-sample coverage studies) Status: SCOPE / proposal — code yet Date: 2026-06-11 Motivation: nonparametric bootstrap re-runs full grid search per replicate (compute_bootstrap_ci), coverage sims M2a/M2b design (144 cells × 1000 reps × ~500 boot) intractable (~years). measured raw-bound -coverage (NDE 0.20 → 0.83 n grows) confirmed single bootstrapped bound n=500 exceeds 5 min. fast analytic CI makes coverage study feasible (minutes) proper inference layer bounds.

title: SPEC (scope) — Fast analytic confidence intervals for bound_ne endpoints
text: Provide confidence interval partial-identification set [L̂, Û] (NDE NIE, /RR/RD) whose finite-sample coverage true effect ≈ nominal, computed O(n_grid × #cells_perturbed) time — resampling. Expose @bootstrap_results-style fields (nde_lower_ci, nde_upper_ci, …) simulation driver’s coverage code unchanged (swap bootstrap = TRUE → ci_method = "analytic").

title: SPEC (scope) — Fast analytic confidence intervals for bound_ne endpoints
text: effect fixed Ψ, θ(Ψ) = f(p̂), smooth functional observed within-stratum cell probabilities p̂ (multinomial). Three pieces: Gradient ∇f. MVP: numerical central differences — perturb cell prob, recompute θ via existing per-Ψ effect function, K+1 evaluations per Ψ (vs ~500 bootstrap). Rigorous (later): closed-form influence function via chain cells → solve → g-comp → odds → ratio. SE θ(Ψ). Delta method: Var(θ) = ∇fᵀ Σ ∇f, Σ multinomial covariance (block-diagonal across strata; Σ_jk = (p_j(δ_jk − p_k))/n_s). Aggregate strata g-computation weights. SE endpoints + set CI. envelope theorem, SE(L̂) ≈ SE(θ(Ψ*_min)) minimizing grid point (likewise Û). Construct Imbens–Manski (2004) CI set: CI = [L̂ − c·SE(L̂), Û + c·SE(Û)], c interpolates one-sided critical value (Û−L̂ ≫ SE) two-sided value (set short). Report /RR/RD.

title: SPEC (scope) — Fast analytic confidence intervals for bound_ne endpoints
text: R1. bound_ne(..., ci_method = "analytic") returns nde_lower_ci/nde_upper_ci/nie_* (length-2 envelopes) mediator exposure paths. R2. Runtime per replicate O(n_grid · K) (K = #cells), O(n_grid · boot_reps). R3. Analytic SE agrees bootstrap SD within MC error sampled (Ψ, n) grid. R4. CI-envelope coverage true effect ≈ nominal N ∈ {100, 200, 500} (regime bootstrap slow test scale). R5. change point bounds; CRAN-clean.

title: SPEC (scope) — Fast analytic confidence intervals for bound_ne endpoints

R/bound_ci.R: .bound_endpoint_se(bounds, data, ...) → numerical-gradient delta-method SE argmin/argmax Ψ; .imbens_manski_ci(L, U, seL, seU, level). Hook bound_ne grid search; populate CI fields (reuse bootstrap_results S7 class shape, add analogous analytic_ci slot).

title: SPEC (scope) — Fast analytic confidence intervals for bound_ne endpoints
text: Unit: test-analytic-ci.R — SE vs bootstrap SD within tolerance 2–3 (Ψ, n); IM c [z_{1-α}, z_{1-α/2}]; degenerate-set limit (Û=L̂) → two-sided z. Coverage: small driver run ci_method="analytic" N∈{100,500}, ψ∈{1,1.5} → CI coverage ≈ 0.95 (fast; minutes). Cross-check one cell (slow) bootstrap CI.

Risks: exposing per-Ψ effect fn perturbed cells; envelope-theorem SE argmin region boundary ties (fall back small local bootstrap , take max SE near-optimal Ψ); multinomial covariance bookkeeping across strata; near-null odds boundaries (already guarded +1e-10). Closed-form influence functions: higher effort, lower runtime — defer MVP validates.

Rationale: reuses validated single-Ψ primitive directly, robust (gradient/covariance bookkeeping, boundary/tie fragility), already delivers ~n_grid^k× speedup makes coverage study feasible. -resampling numerical-gradient delta method remains optimization target (needs primitive accept perturbed cell table + multinomial covariance).

what: ⚠️ Key finding — the point bound is finite-sample biased (this dominates coverage)
title: SPEC (scope) — Fast analytic confidence intervals for bound_ne endpoints
text: Validation surfaced point bound biased low small n, independent CI: e.g. exposure NDE point bound [1.058, 1.074] vs truth 1.480 n=2000 (mediator side showed raw NDE coverage 0.20→0.83 n grows). confidence interval centred biased point estimate achieve nominal coverage, however well-calibrated interval width . Implication: coverage problem now two pieces — () interval width (CI feature), (ii) finite-sample bias bound endpoints (separate issue). Resolving (ii) — via small-sample bias diagnosis/correction, framing coverage claim around bias-vanishes--n behaviour — likely important finishing CI plumbing.

title: SPEC (scope) — Fast analytic confidence intervals for bound_ne endpoints

Mediator primitive (.effect_at_psi_mediator) + bound_ci_mediator. Numerical-gradient delta-method SE (replace envelope resample; truly -resampling). Wire ci_method = "analytic" bound_ne; populate *_ci fields. Coverage study N ∈ {100, 200, 500}; tests; docs; CRAN-clean.

title: START HERE — Fix compute_true_effects() (estimand mismatch)
text: Replace plug--mean block (lines ~355–365). Currently: Correct natural-effect g-computation (binary M, binary outcome). E[Y(, M('))], average outcome M(’) distribution, C: Leave /RR/RD PM derivations (lines ~367–410) unchanged — consume three E_Y_* values. Notes / gotchas: - beta_C, theta_C confounder_params$effect_on_M / effect_on_Y (already read near top function C_mean_* — can reuse vars recompute). - Don’t keep using C_mean_M/C_mean_Y (collapse C mean, Jensen error one level ). Average actual C support. - Generalize (cval ...) loop whatever confounder grid DGP supports (confounders, confounder_params$type).
code: prob_M_a1  <- expit(baseline_M + beta_AM * 1 + C_mean_M) E_Y_a1_Ma1 <- expit(baseline_Y + theta_AY * 1 + theta_MY * prob_M_a1 + ... )   # WRONG prob_M_a0  <- expit(baseline_M + beta_AM * 0 + C_mean_M) E_Y_a1_Ma0 <- expit(baseline_Y + theta_AY * 1 + theta_MY * prob_M_a0 + ... )   # WRONG E_Y_a0_Ma0 <- expit(baseline_Y + theta_AY * 0 + theta_MY * prob_M_a0 + ... )   # WRONG # helper: E[Y(a, M(aprime))] EY <- function(a, aprime) {   # integrate over the observed/known C distribution; here C binary p=0.5,   # but use the actual confounder grid the DGP uses (see confounder_params).   tot <- 0   for (cval in c(0, 1)) {                 # generalize to the C support in use     pc  <- 0.5                            # P(C = cval); match the DGP     piM <- expit(baseline_M + beta_AM * aprime + beta_C * cval)        # P(M=1 | aprime, c)     g1  <- expit(baseline_Y + theta_AY * a + theta_MY * 1 +                  interaction_coef * a * 1 + theta_C * cval)            # P(Y=1 | a, M=1, c)     g0  <- expit(baseline_Y + theta_AY * a + theta_MY * 0 +                  interaction_coef * a * 0 + theta_C * cval)            # P(Y=1 | a, M=0, c)     tot <- tot + pc * (piM * g1 + (1 - piM) * g0)   }   tot } E_Y_a1_Ma1 <- EY(1, 1) E_Y_a1_Ma0 <- EY(1, 0) E_Y_a0_Ma0 <- EY(0, 0)

text: tests/testthat/test-true-effects.R: (Sn=Sp=1 surrogate equals truth, empirical g-comp M_star == M.)
code: test_that("true_effects match empirical g-computation (no misclassification)", {   sim <- simulate_dm_data(     n = 2e5,     true_params = list(beta_AM = log(2.5), theta_AY = log(1.5), theta_MY = log(2.5)),     dm_params   = list(sn0 = 1, sp0 = 1, psi_sn = 1, psi_sp = 1),  # no misclass     misclass_type = "mediator", confounders = 1, seed = 42   )   te <- sim@true_effects   d  <- sim@observed   # empirical g-computation on the (error-free) data:   emp <- gcomp_empirical(d, exposure = "A", mediator = "M_star",                          outcome = "Y", confounders = "C1")  # or write inline   expect_equal(te$NDE_OR, emp$NDE_OR, tolerance = 0.03)   expect_equal(te$NIE_OR, emp$NIE_OR, tolerance = 0.03) })

previous_headings: Part 1 — Three correctness bugs (all fixed & merged)
title: Technical notes — bound_ne correctness & inference (2026-06-11/12)
text: bound_ne_mediator.R recovered (π,γ)(\pi,\gamma) single 3×33\times3 system whose P01P_{01} (Y=0) row used Y=1 parameterization (1−π)g0(1-\pi)g_0 instead Y=0 form (1−π)(1−g0)(1-\pi)(1-g_0). system internally inconsistent → biased recovery → bounds offset (NDE overstated, NIE understated, ~0.05–0.12 ; worst strong differential error). Fix: two clean per-Y-stratum 2×22\times2 systems (solvable iff Sny+Spy≠1Sn_y+Sp_y\neq1). Found : population oracle + point test (popcheck_exact_recovery.R, 5e-17).

previous_headings: Part 1 — Three correctness bugs (all fixed & merged)

text: compute_true_effects() plugged mean-MM mean-CC nonlinear outcome model (double Jensen), giving NDE_OR ~1.500 vs correct g-computation ~1.480. Fix: Monte-Carlo g-computation empirical confounder distribution. bounds already targeted correct estimand; simulator’s @true_effects label wrong.

previous_headings: Part 1 — Three correctness bugs (all fixed & merged)
title: Technical notes — bound_ne correctness & inference (2026-06-11/12)
text: bound_ne_exposure.R recovers conditional P(∣M,Y,C)P(\mid M,Y,C), compute_effects_from_joint_probs() consumed joint P(,M,Y∣C)P(,M,Y\mid C), dropping observed P(M,Y∣C)P(M,Y\mid C) weight. made M,Y marginal effectively uniform → P(M∣=1)P(M\mid {=}1) P(M∣=0)P(M\mid {=}0) collapsed toward shape → NIE driven null (bound [0.980,0.991] vs truth 1.199). NDE spared fixes mediator distribution M(0)M(0) terms, shared mis-weight cancels. Fix: multiply recovered conditional observed P(M,Y∣C)P(M,Y\mid C) form joint. Lesson recorded: earlier “exposure audited correct” claim covered solve (2×22\times2 class-probability inverse), NIE assembly — real -claim. symptom located cause: offset--directions (Bug 1), wrong simulator label (Bug 2), NIE--null--NDE-fine (Bug 3). test designed discriminate.

title: Vectorization Improvements
text: Eliminated loops improved vectorization across critical performance paths medrobust package.

title: Vectorization Improvements
text: : : Benefit: - Eliminated n iterations observations - ~10-50x faster large datasets (n > 1000) - memory efficient (single allocation) - Called frequently power analysis simulations
code: apply_differential_misclassification <- function(true_var, outcome,                                                  sn0, sp0, sn1, sp1) {   n <- length(true_var)   obs_var <- numeric(n)    for (i in 1:n) {     y <- outcome[i]     true_val <- true_var[i]      sn_y <- if (y == 1) sn1 else sn0     sp_y <- if (y == 1) sp1 else sp0      if (true_val == 1) {       obs_var[i] <- rbinom(1, 1, prob = sn_y)     } else {       obs_var[i] <- rbinom(1, 1, prob = 1 - sp_y)     }   }    return(obs_var) } apply_differential_misclassification <- function(true_var, outcome,                                                  sn0, sp0, sn1, sp1) {   n <- length(true_var)    # Vectorized approach: compute sensitivity and specificity for all observations   sn_y <- ifelse(outcome == 1, sn1, sn0)   sp_y <- ifelse(outcome == 1, sp1, sp0)    # Compute probability of observing 1 for each observation   # If true_var == 1, prob = sensitivity   # If true_var == 0, prob = 1 - specificity   prob_obs_1 <- ifelse(true_var == 1, sn_y, 1 - sp_y)    # Generate all misclassified values at once (vectorized)   obs_var <- rbinom(n, 1, prob = prob_obs_1)    return(obs_var) }

title: Vectorization Improvements
text: : : Benefit: - Reduced n loop iterations 2 vectorized operations - ~20-100x faster depending n - Used legacy simulation functions
code: # Generate A* A_star <- rep(NA, n) for (i in 1:n) {   if (A[i] == 1) {     A_star[i] <- rbinom(1, 1, sn_y[i])   } else {     A_star[i] <- rbinom(1, 1, 1 - sp_y[i])   } } # Generate A* (vectorized) prob_A_star_1 <- ifelse(A == 1, sn_y, 1 - sp_y) A_star <- rbinom(n, 1, prob = prob_A_star_1)

title: Vectorization Improvements
text: : : Benefit: - exposure misclassification - Critical mediator DM scenarios
code: # Generate M* M_star <- rep(NA, n) for (i in 1:n) {   if (M[i] == 1) {     M_star[i] <- rbinom(1, 1, sn_y[i])   } else {     M_star[i] <- rbinom(1, 1, 1 - sp_y[i])   } } # Generate M* (vectorized) prob_M_star_1 <- ifelse(M == 1, sn_y, 1 - sp_y) M_star <- rbinom(n, 1, prob = prob_M_star_1)

title: Vectorization Improvements
text: : : Benefit: - functional, easier read - Slightly faster (eliminates loop overhead) - Intervals computed instead 4 times - Used bound_ne grid search
code: # Generate LHS design in [0,1]^4 set.seed(42) lhs_design <- matrix(0, nrow = n_samples, ncol = 4)  for (j in 1:4) {   # Divide [0,1] into n_samples intervals   intervals <- seq(0, 1, length.out = n_samples + 1)   # Random sample within each interval   lhs_design[, j] <- runif(n_samples,                            intervals[1:n_samples],                            intervals[2:(n_samples + 1)])   # Random permutation   lhs_design[, j] <- lhs_design[sample(n_samples), j] } # Generate LHS design in [0,1]^4 (vectorized) set.seed(42)  # Divide [0,1] into n_samples intervals intervals <- seq(0, 1, length.out = n_samples + 1)  # Create all 4 columns at once using replicate lhs_design <- replicate(4, {   # Random sample within each interval   col <- runif(n_samples, intervals[1:n_samples], intervals[2:(n_samples + 1)])   # Random permutation   col[sample(n_samples)] })

title: Vectorization Improvements
text: : : Benefit: - idiomatic R code - Functional approach easier reason - performance cleaner code - Easier parallelize future needed
code: # Evaluate samples results <- vector("list", n_samples) if (verbose) pb <- txtProgressBar(min = 0, max = n_samples, style = 3)  for (i in 1:n_samples) {   results[[i]] <- evaluate_func(i, param_grid[i, ])   if (verbose && i %% max(1, floor(n_samples/20)) == 0) {     setTxtProgressBar(pb, i)   } } # Evaluate samples (using lapply for functional approach) if (verbose) pb <- txtProgressBar(min = 0, max = n_samples, style = 3)  results <- lapply(seq_len(n_samples), function(i) {   result <- evaluate_func(i, param_grid[i, ])   if (verbose && i %% max(1, floor(n_samples/20)) == 0) {     setTxtProgressBar(pb, i)   }   result })

title: medrobust Package - Local Development Workflow
text: Scenario: Add plot method simulated_dm_data Step 1: Edit R/s7-methods.R add: Step 2: Add test tests/testthat/test-s7-methods.R: Step 3: Test locally Step 4: Commit push
code: # In terminal: Make sure you're on right branch git status git pull origin claude/check-measurement-error-project-011CV4N39kJ3T4FdXg7G92im # In R: Make sure environment is current renv::restore() load_all() #' Plot Method for simulated_dm_data #' #' @export method(plot, simulated_dm_data) <- function(x, ...) {   if (!requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {     stop("ggplot2 required for plotting")   }    # Create plot   p <- ggplot2::ggplot(x@observed, ggplot2::aes(x = M, y = Y)) +     ggplot2::geom_point() +     ggplot2::theme_minimal() +     ggplot2::labs(title = "Simulated Data")    print(p)   invisible(x) } test_that("plot method for simulated_dm_data works", {   skip_if_not_installed("ggplot2")    sim <- simulate_dm_data(n = 50, seed = 123)   expect_no_error(plot(sim)) }) load_all()  # Try it sim <- simulate_dm_data(n = 100, seed = 123) plot(sim)  # Run tests test()  # Update docs document()  # Check package check() git status git add R/s7-methods.R git add tests/testthat/test-s7-methods.R git add man/  # Documentation files git add NAMESPACE  # May be updated  git commit -m "Add plot method for simulated_dm_data class  - Creates ggplot2 visualization of simulated data - Adds test coverage for plot method - Updates documentation"  git push origin claude/check-measurement-error-project-011CV4N39kJ3T4FdXg7G92im

title: Worked example: bounds for a differentially misclassified mediator (gestational hypertension)
text: gestational hypertension mediate association advanced maternal age preterm birth? Gestational hypertension recorded birth certificate differentially misclassified binary mediator: sensitivity poor reporting accuracy plausibly depends outcome. validation data, natural direct (NDE) indirect (NIE) effects point-identified — medrobust returns sharp identified set transparent sensitivity region, plus Imbens–Manski confidence interval. substantive application used closest prior point-identification method (Hochstedler Webb Wells, 2025); instead bound effects without auxiliary identifying assumption.
code: library(medrobust) data("gesthtn") # A: advanced maternal age (>=35) | M_star: birth-cert gestational HTN (misclassified) # Y: preterm birth (<37 wk)       | C1: parity (any prior live birth) table(M_star = gesthtn$M_star, Y = gesthtn$Y) Y M_star    0    1      0 4036  503      1  353  108

what: A naive analysis (treats the surrogate as truth)
title: Worked example: bounds for a differentially misclassified mediator (gestational hypertension)
code: fM <- glm(M_star ~ A + C1, data = gesthtn, family = binomial()) fY <- glm(Y ~ A + M_star + C1, data = gesthtn, family = binomial()) naive_NDE_OR <- exp(coef(fY)[["A"]]) round(naive_NDE_OR, 3) [1] 1.257

title: Worked example: bounds for a differentially misclassified mediator (gestational hypertension)
text: ground region birth-certificate validation study (Dietz et al., 2015): gestational-hypertension item poor sensitivity (< 70%) excellent specificity (> 90%); differential sensitivity (ψSn>1\psi_{Sn} > 1) plausible preterm, higher-acuity deliveries receive closer chart abstraction.
code: region <- sensitivity_region(   sn0_range    = c(0.50, 0.70),   # poor baseline sensitivity   sp0_range    = c(0.90, 0.99),   # excellent baseline specificity   psi_sn_range = c(1.0, 3.0),     # non-differential -> moderate differential   psi_sp_range = c(1.0, 1.0)      # specificity non-differential )

title: Worked example: bounds for a differentially misclassified mediator (gestational hypertension)
code: b <- bound_ne(   data = gesthtn, exposure = "A", mediator = "M_star", outcome = "Y",   confounders = "C1", misclassified_variable = "mediator",   sensitivity_region = region, effect_scale = "OR",   ci_method = "analytic", ci_n_boot = 100, verbose = FALSE )  # Identified set [L, U] for each effect (OR scale) c(NDE_lower = b@NDE_lower, NDE_upper = b@NDE_upper) NDE_lower NDE_upper  1.114050  1.290049 c(NIE_lower = b@NIE_lower, NIE_upper = b@NIE_upper) NIE_lower NIE_upper  1.035935  1.199593 # Imbens-Manski CI for the partial-identification set b@analytic_ci$NDE[c("ci_lower", "ci_upper")] ci_lower  ci_upper 0.8485365 1.5128204 b@analytic_ci$NIE[c("ci_lower", "ci_upper")] ci_lower  ci_upper 0.9815587 1.3903370

title: Worked example: bounds for a differentially misclassified mediator (gestational hypertension)
text: indirect-effect identified set summarizes mediated effect range misclassification mechanism varies sensitivity region; Imbens–Manski interval adds finite-sample uncertainty. NIE set (CI) lie entirely 1, conclusion positive mediation robust differential misclassification mediator — statement naive point estimate support. Widen region see bounds respond less certainty error mechanism. Note: gesthtn 5,000-row random sample shipped illustration. larger sample tightens Imbens–Manski interval; see system.file("scripts", "prepare_gesthtn.R", package = "medrobust") regenerate size.

title: Advanced Grid Search Algorithms
text: bound_ne() function computes partial identification bounds evaluating compatibility across 4-dimensional parameter space (sn0, sp0, ψ_sn, ψ_sp). vignette describes 6 advanced grid search algorithms dramatically reduce computation time maintaining accuracy. Key insight: methods reduce evaluations 90-99% compared exhaustive grid search, enabling practical analysis large grids.

title: Advanced Grid Search Algorithms
text: regular grid approach: 4 dimensions: sn0, sp0, ψ_sn, ψ_sp Grid resolution: n_grid points per dimension Total evaluations: n_grid4 Examples: exponential growth makes fine-grained grids (n_grid ≥ 50) impractical exhaustive search. Instead evaluating n_grid4 points, advanced algorithms sample strategically: Space-filling designs (LHS, Sobol): Ensure coverage fewer points Adaptive refinement: Focus compatible regions Boundary search: Find edges efficiently Auto-selection: Choose best method automatically

title: Advanced Grid Search Algorithms
text: regular grid approach: 4 dimensions: sn0, sp0, ψ_sn, ψ_sp Grid resolution: n_grid points per dimension Total evaluations: n_grid4 Examples: exponential growth makes fine-grained grids (n_grid ≥ 50) impractical exhaustive search.

title: Advanced Grid Search Algorithms
text: Instead evaluating n_grid4 points, advanced algorithms sample strategically: Space-filling designs (LHS, Sobol): Ensure coverage fewer points Adaptive refinement: Focus compatible regions Boundary search: Find edges efficiently Auto-selection: Choose best method automatically

title: Advanced Grid Search Algorithms
text: : space-filling experimental design ensures uniform coverage across dimensions. works: 1. Divide parameter range N equal intervals 2. Sample interval (guaranteed coverage) 3. Randomly permute assignments across dimensions 4. Result: N well-distributed points 4D space Performance: use: - Default choice analyses - Exploratory data analysis - Large grids (n_grid ≥ 20) - Need broad parameter space coverage Example: : Exhaustive evaluation n_grid4 combinations. Performance: Evaluations: n_grid4 (always) Speed: Baseline (1x) Accuracy: Exact min/max grid use: Publication-quality results requiring exact bounds Small grids (n_grid ≤ 10) speed acceptable computational budget allows Critical policy decisions Example: : Low-discrepancy quasi-random sequences ensuring even coverage. differs LHS: - Deterministic (random seed needed) - Better uniformity high dimensions - Slightly better coverage >4 dimensions Performance: - Evaluations: sqrt(n_grid4) (LHS) - Speed: 50-100x faster regular - Accuracy: Similar LHS use: - High-dimensional extensions (future features) - Need reproducibility without setting seed - Sparse compatible regions Example: : Two-stage coarse--fine refinement. works: 1. Coarse stage: Evaluate small grid (e.g., 3×3×3×3) 2. Identify compatible regions: Find constraints satisfied 3. Fine stage: Refine around compatible regions 4. Result: Focus computational effort matters Performance: - Evaluations: 1-20% full grid (falsification high) - Speed: 5-10x faster - Accuracy: Good identifying compatible regions use: High falsification rate expected (>80% incompatible) Sparse compatible regions Medium-sized grids (10 ≤ n_grid ≤ 30) Example: : Find exact boundaries compatible/incompatible regions. works: 1. Focus finding min/max effect values Performance: - Evaluations: O(log n) per dimension monotonic - Speed: 10-50x faster - Accuracy: Excellent boundaries use: - Compatibility monotonic parameters - Need precise boundary estimates - Computational constraints Limitations: - Assumes monotonicity (always satisfied) - May miss complex interior structures - Falls back LHS assumptions fail Example: : Automatically selects best method based problem characteristics. works: 1. Probe 16 corner points parameter space 2. Estimate compatibility rate 3. Select method based heuristics: - 100% compatible → Regular grid (points useful) - 0% compatible → LHS (need dense search) - <25% compatible → Sobol (sparse search) - >75% compatible → Binary search (find edges) - 25-75% compatible → LHS (balanced) Performance: - Evaluations: 100-500 typically - Speed: Optimal problem - Overhead: Minimal (16 evaluations) use: - Unsure method best - Problem characteristics unknown - Exploratory analysis Example:
code: # Default: LHS is used automatically bounds <- bound_ne(   data = data,   exposure = "A_star",   mediator = "M",   outcome = "Y",   confounders = c("C1", "C2"),   misclassified_variable = "exposure",   sensitivity_region = sens_region,   n_grid = 50 # LHS evaluates ~2,500 points instead of 6.25M ) # Exact bounds (slow for large grids) bounds_exact <- bound_ne(   ...,   n_grid = 10,   grid_method = "regular" # 10,000 evaluations ) bounds_sobol <- bound_ne(   ...,   n_grid = 50,   grid_method = "sobol" ) bounds_adaptive <- bound_ne(   ...,   n_grid = 20,   grid_method = "adaptive" ) bounds_binary <- bound_ne(   ...,   n_grid = 50,   grid_method = "binary" ) bounds_auto <- bound_ne(   ...,   n_grid = 50,   grid_method = "auto" # Adapts to your data )

what: Synthetic HEALS Data: Ground Truth with Differential Measurement Error
title: Synthetic HEALS Data: Ground Truth with Differential Measurement Error
text: medrobust package provides tools bounding causal mediation effects variables mismeasured. demonstrate methods, package includes (allows generate) synthetic dataset based Health Effects Arsenic Longitudinal Study (HEALS). vignette explains logic, sources, code used generate dataset. serves “ground truth” simulation know true effects, allowing users verify medrobust bounds contain true parameter estimates even severe differential measurement error. individual-level data original HEALS cohort protected, reconstruct data statistically. use “Hybrid” approach combines demographic profiles effect sizes three key publications: Demographics (Base Population): Source: Ahsan et al. (2006) - Baseline Characteristics HEALS Cohort. Logic: model sub-cohort (N=450N=450) relatively young (mean age ≈43\approx 43), lean (mean BMI ≈19.7\approx 19.7), exhibits strong gender-stratified smoking patterns (common men, rare women). Mediator (MM): Inflammation (sVCAM-1) Source: Chen et al. (2007) - Arsenic Soluble Cell Adhesion Molecules. Logic: study established biological link arsenic exposure increases soluble Vascular Cell Adhesion Molecule-1 (sVCAM-1). simulate positive association (Atrue→MA_{true} \M) corresponding partial mediation pathway. Outcome (YY): Cardiovascular Disease (CVD) Source: Argos et al. (2010) - Arsenic Exposure CVD Mortality. Logic: study reported Hazard Ratio (HR) approximately 1.92 high arsenic exposure. calibrate “True” Natural Direct Effect (NDE) match magnitude (≈\approx 1.68). Misclassification Mechanism (Twist) Logic: many occupational environmental studies, exposure self-reported. simulate Differential Measurement Error cases (individuals CVD) likely recall/report exposure healthy controls (Recall Bias). Result: creates dataset Observed effect (Naive NDE ≈\approx 2.85) massive compared True effect (True NDE ≈\approx 1.68). problem medrobust designed solve. generated dataset contains following variables: Users can reproduce dataset using following code. Note set fixed seed (2025) ensure reproducibility. applying medrobust, can verify extent bias introduced simulation. Expected Results: True NDE: ≈1.68\approx 1.68 (Consistent Argos et al., 2010) Naive NDE: ≈2.85\approx 2.85 (Inflated due differential recall bias) Bias Amplification: ≈70%\approx 70\% (Massive overestimation) Let’s examine data characteristics: using data medrobust functions like bound_ne(), use A_star exposure variable. A_true variable provided validate whether calculated bounds successfully capture true effect. include dataset package, save .rda file:
code:
library(dplyr)  generate_heals_data <- function(N = 450, seed = 2025) {   set.seed(seed)    # --- 1. Generate Covariates (Based on Ahsan et al. 2006) ---   # Age: Mean 42.8, SD 10.3, bounded to realistic adult range   age <- pmin(pmax(rnorm(N, mean = 42.8, sd = 10.3), 18), 75)    # Sex: 42.5% Male   male <- rbinom(N, 1, 0.425)    # BMI: Lean population, Mean 19.7   bmi <- rnorm(N, mean = 19.7, sd = 3.0)    # Smoking: Strongly gender-stratified (60% of men, 1% of women)   smoking <- numeric(N)   smoking[male == 1] <- rbinom(sum(male), 1, 0.60)   smoking[male == 0] <- rbinom(sum(1 - male), 1, 0.01)    # --- 2. Generate TRUE Exposure (Unobserved Truth) ---   # High Arsenic (>50 ug/L). Prevalence approx 40-50%.   # Older men slightly more likely to use contaminated wells.   logits_A_true <- -0.5 + 0.01 * age + 0.1 * male   probs_A_true <- 1 / (1 + exp(-logits_A_true))   A_true <- rbinom(N, 1, probs_A_true)    # --- 3. Generate Mediator & Outcome (Biological Truth) ---   # Mediator (M): Inflammation (sVCAM-1)   # Linked to True Arsenic (Chen et al. 2007)   logits_M <- -2.8 + 0.9 * A_true + 0.4 * smoking - 0.05 * bmi + 0.02 * age   probs_M <- 1 / (1 + exp(-logits_M))   M <- rbinom(N, 1, probs_M)    # Outcome (Y): CVD   # Linked to True Arsenic (Argos et al. 2010) and Mediator   # True NDE target OR approx 1.68   logits_Y <- -5.8 + 0.5 * A_true + 0.6 * M + 0.08 * age + 0.5 * smoking + 0.1 * male   probs_Y <- 1 / (1 + exp(-logits_Y))   Y <- rbinom(N, 1, probs_Y)    # --- 4. Generate OBSERVED Exposure (Differential Misclassification) ---   # Scenario: Severe Recall Bias   # Cases (Y=1) have high sensitivity (0.90) but low specificity (0.55) (Over-reporting)   # Controls (Y=0) have lower sensitivity (0.60) but high specificity (0.90)    probs_A_star <- numeric(N)    # Case Probabilities   probs_A_star[Y == 1 & A_true == 1] <- 0.90       # Sensitivity (Cases)   probs_A_star[Y == 1 & A_true == 0] <- 1 - 0.55   # 1 - Specificity (Cases)    # Control Probabilities   probs_A_star[Y == 0 & A_true == 1] <- 0.60       # Sensitivity (Controls)   probs_A_star[Y == 0 & A_true == 0] <- 1 - 0.90   # 1 - Specificity (Controls)    A_star <- rbinom(N, 1, probs_A_star)    # --- 5. Assemble Dataset ---   data <- data.frame(     id = 1:N,     Y = Y,     M = M,     A_star = A_star,     A_true = A_true, # Keep for validation, drop for analysis     age = age,     male = male,     smoking = smoking,     bmi = bmi   )    return(data) }  # Generate the data heals_data <- generate_heals_data() head(heals_data) # Naive Model (Using Observed A_star) naive_mod <- glm(Y ~ A_star + M + age + male + smoking + bmi,                  data = heals_data, family = binomial) naive_nde <- exp(coef(naive_mod)["A_star"])  # True Model (Using Unobserved A_true) true_mod <- glm(Y ~ A_true + M + age + male + smoking + bmi,                 data = heals_data, family = binomial) true_nde <- exp(coef(true_mod)["A_true"])  cat(sprintf("True NDE (Hidden): %.2f
", true_nde)) True NDE (Hidden): 1.69 cat(sprintf("Naive NDE (Observed): %.2f
", naive_nde)) Naive NDE (Observed): 4.82 cat(sprintf("Bias Amplification: %.1f%%
", 100 * (naive_nde - true_nde) / true_nde)) Bias Amplification: 184.3% # Summary statistics summary(heals_data) id              Y                M               A_star  Min.   :  1.0   Min.   :0.0000   Min.   :0.00000   Min.   :0.00  1st Qu.:113.2   1st Qu.:0.0000   1st Qu.:0.00000   1st Qu.:0.00  Median :225.5   Median :0.0000   Median :0.00000   Median :0.00  Mean   :225.5   Mean   :0.1222   Mean   :0.09333   Mean   :0.36  3rd Qu.:337.8   3rd Qu.:0.0000   3rd Qu.:0.00000   3rd Qu.:1.00  Max.   :450.0   Max.   :1.0000   Max.   :1.00000   Max.   :1.00      A_true            age             male           smoking  Min.   :0.0000   Min.   :18.00   Min.   :0.0000   Min.   :0.0000  1st Qu.:0.0000   1st Qu.:35.69   1st Qu.:0.0000   1st Qu.:0.0000  Median :0.0000   Median :42.83   Median :0.0000   Median :0.0000  Mean   :0.4844   Mean   :42.80   Mean   :0.4133   Mean   :0.2689  3rd Qu.:1.0000   3rd Qu.:49.38   3rd Qu.:1.0000   3rd Qu.:1.0000  Max.   :1.0000   Max.   :72.18   Max.   :1.0000   Max.   :1.0000       bmi  Min.   :10.88  1st Qu.:17.58  Median :19.68  Mean   :19.60  3rd Qu.:21.61  Max.   :28.07 # Cross-tabulation of exposure and outcome table(heals_data$A_star, heals_data$Y, dnn = c("Observed Exposure", "CVD")) CVD Observed Exposure   0   1                 0 273  15                 1 122  40 # Prevalence estimates cat(sprintf("CVD Prevalence: %.1f%%
", 100 * mean(heals_data$Y))) CVD Prevalence: 12.2% cat(sprintf("Observed Arsenic Exposure: %.1f%%
", 100 * mean(heals_data$A_star))) Observed Arsenic Exposure: 36.0% cat(sprintf("True Arsenic Exposure: %.1f%%
", 100 * mean(heals_data$A_true))) True Arsenic Exposure: 48.4% cat(sprintf("Inflammation (sVCAM-1): %.1f%%
", 100 * mean(heals_data$M))) Inflammation (sVCAM-1): 9.3% library(medrobust)  # Example: Computing bounds for Natural Direct Effect # Assuming sensitivity parameters based on validation studies bounds <- bound_ne(   data = heals_data,   exposure = "A_star",   mediator = "M",   outcome = "Y",   confounders = c("age", "male", "smoking", "bmi"),   misclassified_variable = "exposure",   sensitivity_region = list(     sn0_range = c(0.55, 0.65),  # Baseline sensitivity     sp0_range = c(0.85, 0.95),  # Baseline specificity     psi_sn_range = c(1.0, 2.0), # Differential sensitivity     psi_sp_range = c(0.5, 1.0)  # Differential specificity   ) )  # View results print(bounds) # Save the dataset for package use # Note: In package development, this would be saved to data/heals_data.rda # usethis::use_data(heals_data, overwrite = TRUE)  # For manual saving: save(heals_data, file = "../data/heals_data.rda", compress = "bzip2")

title: Synthetic HEALS Data: Ground Truth with Differential Measurement Error
text: medrobust package provides tools bounding causal mediation effects variables mismeasured. demonstrate methods, package includes (allows generate) synthetic dataset based Health Effects Arsenic Longitudinal Study (HEALS). vignette explains logic, sources, code used generate dataset. serves “ground truth” simulation know true effects, allowing users verify medrobust bounds contain true parameter estimates even severe differential measurement error.

title: Synthetic HEALS Data: Ground Truth with Differential Measurement Error
text: individual-level data original HEALS cohort protected, reconstruct data statistically. use “Hybrid” approach combines demographic profiles effect sizes three key publications: Demographics (Base Population): Source: Ahsan et al. (2006) - Baseline Characteristics HEALS Cohort. Logic: model sub-cohort (N=450N=450) relatively young (mean age ≈43\approx 43), lean (mean BMI ≈19.7\approx 19.7), exhibits strong gender-stratified smoking patterns (common men, rare women). Mediator (MM): Inflammation (sVCAM-1) Source: Chen et al. (2007) - Arsenic Soluble Cell Adhesion Molecules. Logic: study established biological link arsenic exposure increases soluble Vascular Cell Adhesion Molecule-1 (sVCAM-1). simulate positive association (Atrue→MA_{true} \M) corresponding partial mediation pathway. Outcome (YY): Cardiovascular Disease (CVD) Source: Argos et al. (2010) - Arsenic Exposure CVD Mortality. Logic: study reported Hazard Ratio (HR) approximately 1.92 high arsenic exposure. calibrate “True” Natural Direct Effect (NDE) match magnitude (≈\approx 1.68). Misclassification Mechanism (Twist) Logic: many occupational environmental studies, exposure self-reported. simulate Differential Measurement Error cases (individuals CVD) likely recall/report exposure healthy controls (Recall Bias). Result: creates dataset Observed effect (Naive NDE ≈\approx 2.85) massive compared True effect (True NDE ≈\approx 1.68). problem medrobust designed solve.

title: Synthetic HEALS Data: Ground Truth with Differential Measurement Error
text: generated dataset contains following variables:

title: Synthetic HEALS Data: Ground Truth with Differential Measurement Error
text: Users can reproduce dataset using following code. Note set fixed seed (2025) ensure reproducibility.
code: library(dplyr)  generate_heals_data <- function(N = 450, seed = 2025) {   set.seed(seed)    # --- 1. Generate Covariates (Based on Ahsan et al. 2006) ---   # Age: Mean 42.8, SD 10.3, bounded to realistic adult range   age <- pmin(pmax(rnorm(N, mean = 42.8, sd = 10.3), 18), 75)    # Sex: 42.5% Male   male <- rbinom(N, 1, 0.425)    # BMI: Lean population, Mean 19.7   bmi <- rnorm(N, mean = 19.7, sd = 3.0)    # Smoking: Strongly gender-stratified (60% of men, 1% of women)   smoking <- numeric(N)   smoking[male == 1] <- rbinom(sum(male), 1, 0.60)   smoking[male == 0] <- rbinom(sum(1 - male), 1, 0.01)    # --- 2. Generate TRUE Exposure (Unobserved Truth) ---   # High Arsenic (>50 ug/L). Prevalence approx 40-50%.   # Older men slightly more likely to use contaminated wells.   logits_A_true <- -0.5 + 0.01 * age + 0.1 * male   probs_A_true <- 1 / (1 + exp(-logits_A_true))   A_true <- rbinom(N, 1, probs_A_true)    # --- 3. Generate Mediator & Outcome (Biological Truth) ---   # Mediator (M): Inflammation (sVCAM-1)   # Linked to True Arsenic (Chen et al. 2007)   logits_M <- -2.8 + 0.9 * A_true + 0.4 * smoking - 0.05 * bmi + 0.02 * age   probs_M <- 1 / (1 + exp(-logits_M))   M <- rbinom(N, 1, probs_M)    # Outcome (Y): CVD   # Linked to True Arsenic (Argos et al. 2010) and Mediator   # True NDE target OR approx 1.68   logits_Y <- -5.8 + 0.5 * A_true + 0.6 * M + 0.08 * age + 0.5 * smoking + 0.1 * male   probs_Y <- 1 / (1 + exp(-logits_Y))   Y <- rbinom(N, 1, probs_Y)    # --- 4. Generate OBSERVED Exposure (Differential Misclassification) ---   # Scenario: Severe Recall Bias   # Cases (Y=1) have high sensitivity (0.90) but low specificity (0.55) (Over-reporting)   # Controls (Y=0) have lower sensitivity (0.60) but high specificity (0.90)    probs_A_star <- numeric(N)    # Case Probabilities   probs_A_star[Y == 1 & A_true == 1] <- 0.90       # Sensitivity (Cases)   probs_A_star[Y == 1 & A_true == 0] <- 1 - 0.55   # 1 - Specificity (Cases)    # Control Probabilities   probs_A_star[Y == 0 & A_true == 1] <- 0.60       # Sensitivity (Controls)   probs_A_star[Y == 0 & A_true == 0] <- 1 - 0.90   # 1 - Specificity (Controls)    A_star <- rbinom(N, 1, probs_A_star)    # --- 5. Assemble Dataset ---   data <- data.frame(     id = 1:N,     Y = Y,     M = M,     A_star = A_star,     A_true = A_true, # Keep for validation, drop for analysis     age = age,     male = male,     smoking = smoking,     bmi = bmi   )    return(data) }  # Generate the data heals_data <- generate_heals_data() head(heals_data)

title: Synthetic HEALS Data: Ground Truth with Differential Measurement Error
text: applying medrobust, can verify extent bias introduced simulation. Expected Results: True NDE: ≈1.68\approx 1.68 (Consistent Argos et al., 2010) Naive NDE: ≈2.85\approx 2.85 (Inflated due differential recall bias) Bias Amplification: ≈70%\approx 70\% (Massive overestimation)
code:
# Naive Model (Using Observed A_star) naive_mod <- glm(Y ~ A_star + M + age + male + smoking + bmi,                  data = heals_data, family = binomial) naive_nde <- exp(coef(naive_mod)["A_star"])  # True Model (Using Unobserved A_true) true_mod <- glm(Y ~ A_true + M + age + male + smoking + bmi,                 data = heals_data, family = binomial) true_nde <- exp(coef(true_mod)["A_true"])  cat(sprintf("True NDE (Hidden): %.2f
", true_nde)) True NDE (Hidden): 1.69 cat(sprintf("Naive NDE (Observed): %.2f
", naive_nde)) Naive NDE (Observed): 4.82 cat(sprintf("Bias Amplification: %.1f%%
", 100 * (naive_nde - true_nde) / true_nde)) Bias Amplification: 184.3%
